context.description: Reflective prose contrasts dream consciousness with waking awareness and argues that the dreamer cannot grasp the structure of waking life. A section titled 'Up and down, again and again' develops a hypothetical awakening argument through a jungle metaphor and the idea of higher consciousness.
normalization_notes: Translated the visible section heading into English for retrieval.
Promoted the source filename as the recoverable work title.

fields.section_heading: Up and down, again and again

rows.heading_text: Up and down, again and again
rows.text_content: Up and down, again and again

rows.paragraph_text: Dreams can contain many individual components of waking life, but not the structure that lets a person orient action, remember the past, and plan the future. The dreamer’s awareness is narrower than waking awareness, and the page uses a jungle metaphor to explain how waking reality becomes inaccessible in dreams.
rows.text_content: Dreams can contain many individual components of waking life, but not the structure that lets a person orient action, remember the past, and plan the future. The dreamer’s awareness is narrower than waking awareness, and the page uses a jungle metaphor to explain how waking reality becomes inaccessible in dreams.

rows.paragraph_text: A hypothetical speaker presents waking life as a higher state of consciousness and asks whether a dreamer would believe that awakening is possible, arguing that the only obstacle is the dreamer’s own state.
rows.text_content: A hypothetical speaker presents waking life as a higher state of consciousness and asks whether a dreamer would believe that awakening is possible, arguing that the only obstacle is the dreamer’s own state.
